break the ice.meaning: To initiate conversation in a socially awkward situation
break the ice.example: He told a joke to break the ice at the meeting.
break the ice.tip: Imagine literally breaking a frozen surface to start flowing water

bite the bullet.meaning: To endure a painful or difficult situation bravely
bite the bullet.example: She bit the bullet and told him the truth.
bite the bullet.tip: Soldiers used to bite bullets during surgery without anesthesia

let the cat out of the bag.example: She let the cat out of the bag about the surprise party.
let the cat out of the bag.tip: Imagine a cat escaping from a bag — once out, you can't hide it again

hit the nail on the head.meaning: To describe exactly what is causing a situation or problem
hit the nail on the head.example: You hit the nail on the head with that analysis.
hit the nail on the head.tip: A carpenter hitting a nail perfectly — precise and accurate

once in a blue moon.example: He only visits once in a blue moon.
once in a blue moon.tip: Blue moons are rare astronomical events — hence 'rarely'

piece of cake.example: The exam was a piece of cake.
piece of cake.tip: Eating cake is easy and pleasant — so is the task

spill the beans.example: Someone spilled the beans about the merger.
spill the beans.tip: Beans scattering everywhere — secrets spreading uncontrollably

cost an arm and a leg.example: That sports car costs an arm and a leg.
cost an arm and a leg.tip: Giving up body parts = extremely high cost

beat around the bush.meaning: To avoid talking about what is important; to delay
beat around the bush.example: Stop beating around the bush and tell me what happened.
beat around the bush.tip: Hunters used to beat bushes to drive out game — circling, not going straight

under the weather.example: I'm feeling a bit under the weather today.
under the weather.tip: Sailors went below deck in bad weather when sick

burn the midnight oil.meaning: To work late into the night
burn the midnight oil.example: She's been burning the midnight oil to finish the project.
burn the midnight oil.tip: Before electricity, oil lamps were burned for light when working late

the last straw.meaning: The final problem in a series that makes the situation unbearable
the last straw.example: His rude comment was the last straw.
the last straw.tip: From 'the straw that broke the camel's back' — one final tiny weight

turn a blind eye.meaning: To ignore something that you know is wrong
turn a blind eye.example: The manager turned a blind eye to the violations.
turn a blind eye.tip: Admiral Nelson literally put telescope to his blind eye to 'not see' a signal

a blessing in disguise.meaning: Something that seems bad at first but turns out to be good
a blessing in disguise.example: Losing that job was a blessing in disguise.
a blessing in disguise.tip: A good thing wearing a costume — hidden benefit

actions speak louder than words.meaning: What you do is more important than what you say
actions speak louder than words.example: He promised to help, but actions speak louder than words.
actions speak louder than words.tip: Doing is stronger proof than talking

add insult to injury.meaning: To make a bad situation worse
add insult to injury.example: To add insult to injury, it started raining after we got a flat tire.
add insult to injury.tip: First hurt, then humiliated — double damage

back to the drawing board.meaning: To start over because the previous plan failed
back to the drawing board.example: The prototype failed, so it's back to the drawing board.
back to the drawing board.tip: Architects start new designs at their drawing board

barking up the wrong tree.meaning: To make a false assumption or pursue a mistaken course
barking up the wrong tree.example: If you think I stole it, you're barking up the wrong tree.
barking up the wrong tree.tip: A dog barking at a tree where the cat already left

cut corners.meaning: To do something in the easiest or cheapest way, sacrificing quality
cut corners.example: The builder cut corners on the foundation.
cut corners.tip: Taking a shortcut by cutting the corner of a path

get out of hand.example: The argument got out of hand quickly.
get out of hand.tip: Something escaping from your hand — you've lost grip

hang in there.meaning: To persist through difficulty; don't give up
hang in there.example: I know it's hard, but hang in there.
hang in there.tip: Like hanging from a ledge — keep holding on

jump on the bandwagon.meaning: To join a popular trend or activity
jump on the bandwagon.example: Everyone jumped on the bandwagon after the team started winning.
jump on the bandwagon.tip: A parade wagon — jumping on to join the popular crowd

kill two birds with one stone.meaning: To accomplish two things at once
kill two birds with one stone.example: By studying on the train, I kill two birds with one stone.

miss the boat.example: If we don't act now, we'll miss the boat.
miss the boat.tip: The ship has sailed without you

no pain no gain.meaning: You have to work hard and endure difficulty to achieve results
no pain no gain.example: Training is exhausting, but no pain no gain.

on thin ice.meaning: In a risky or dangerous situation
on thin ice.example: After missing two deadlines, she's on thin ice with her boss.
on thin ice.tip: Walking on ice that might crack — one wrong step and you fall

pull someone's leg.meaning: To joke with or tease someone
pull someone's leg.example: I was just pulling your leg — I didn't mean it seriously.
pull someone's leg.tip: Playfully tugging someone's leg to make them stumble

see eye to eye.example: We don't always see eye to eye on politics.
see eye to eye.tip: Looking at each other at the same level — equal perspective

sit on the fence.meaning: To avoid making a decision or taking a side
sit on the fence.example: Stop sitting on the fence and choose.
sit on the fence.tip: Balanced on a fence, not falling to either side

speak of the devil.meaning: Said when the person you were just talking about appears
speak of the devil.example: Speak of the devil — here comes John now!
speak of the devil.tip: In old superstition, speaking of the devil would summon him

steal someone's thunder.meaning: To take credit or attention away from someone
steal someone's thunder.example: She stole my thunder by announcing it first.

take it with a grain of salt.meaning: To not take something too seriously; to be skeptical
take it with a grain of salt.example: Take his advice with a grain of salt.
take it with a grain of salt.tip: A little salt makes something more palatable — adjust your belief

the ball is in your court.meaning: It is your decision or responsibility now
the ball is in your court.example: I've made my offer — the ball is in your court.
the ball is in your court.tip: In tennis, your turn to play

through thick and thin.meaning: Through good times and bad times; loyal in every situation
through thick and thin.example: They've been friends through thick and thin.
through thick and thin.tip: Walking through both dense forest and open ground — never quitting

throw in the towel.example: After three failed attempts, he threw in the towel.
throw in the towel.tip: In boxing, a corner throws a towel to stop the fight

up in the air.example: The plans for next year are still up in the air.
up in the air.tip: Something floating, not grounded or settled

when pigs fly.example: He'll clean his room when pigs fly.
when pigs fly.tip: Pigs can't fly — so the event is impossible

wrap your head around.example: I just can't wrap my head around quantum physics.
wrap your head around.tip: Trying to mentally embrace a complex idea

a dime a dozen.meaning: Very common and of no particular value
a dime a dozen.example: Opinions are a dime a dozen around here.
a dime a dozen.tip: So cheap you could buy twelve for a dime

at the drop of a hat.example: She'd travel at the drop of a hat.
at the drop of a hat.tip: Dropping a hat was an old signal to start something instantly

back to square one.meaning: To go back to the beginning
back to square one.example: The deal collapsed, so we're back to square one.
back to square one.tip: Like a board game — returning to the first square

bend over backwards.meaning: To try very hard to help or please someone
bend over backwards.example: She bent over backwards to make us comfortable.

by the skin of your teeth.meaning: Just barely; by a very narrow margin
by the skin of your teeth.example: He passed the exam by the skin of his teeth.
by the skin of your teeth.tip: Teeth have almost no skin — the thinnest possible margin

cry over spilled milk.meaning: To be upset about something that has already happened and cannot be changed
cry over spilled milk.example: There's no use crying over spilled milk — let's move on.
cry over spilled milk.tip: Once milk is spilled, you can't put it back — so why be upset?

down to earth.example: Despite being famous, she's very down to earth.
down to earth.tip: Feet on the ground, not floating in fantasy

every cloud has a silver lining.meaning: Every bad situation has some positive aspect
every cloud has a silver lining.example: Losing that job led to a better one — every cloud has a silver lining.
every cloud has a silver lining.tip: Even dark clouds have bright edges from the sun behind them

get cold feet.meaning: To become too nervous or afraid to do something
get cold feet.example: He got cold feet before the wedding.
get cold feet.tip: Fear makes your feet cold — you freeze and can't move forward

go the extra mile.meaning: To make more effort than is expected
go the extra mile.example: She always goes the extra mile for her clients.
go the extra mile.tip: Walking further than needed — doing more than the minimum

in the heat of the moment.meaning: Overwhelmed by what is happening at the time
in the heat of the moment.example: I said things I regret in the heat of the moment.
in the heat of the moment.tip: Emotions burning hot — you act on impulse

keep your chin up.meaning: To remain cheerful in a difficult situation
keep your chin up.example: Things will get better — keep your chin up.
keep your chin up.tip: Head held high, not drooping in defeat

leave no stone unturned.meaning: To do everything possible to find or achieve something
leave no stone unturned.example: The police left no stone unturned in their search.
leave no stone unturned.tip: Turning over every stone to look underneath — thorough search

make a long story short.meaning: To get to the point quickly
make a long story short.example: To make a long story short, we missed the flight.
make a long story short.tip: Cutting a long tale to its essential ending

nip it in the bud.meaning: To stop something at an early stage before it becomes worse
nip it in the bud.example: We need to nip this problem in the bud.
nip it in the bud.tip: Cutting a plant bud before it blooms — stopping growth early

on the same page.meaning: To have the same understanding or opinion
on the same page.example: Let's make sure we're all on the same page before we proceed.
on the same page.tip: Everyone reading the same page of a book — shared information

play it by ear.meaning: To decide what to do as things happen, without a plan
play it by ear.example: Let's just play it by ear and see how the day goes.
play it by ear.tip: Musicians playing without written music — improvising

read between the lines.meaning: To understand the hidden meaning behind something said or written
read between the lines.example: If you read between the lines, she was actually asking for help.
read between the lines.tip: The real message is hidden in the spaces, not the words themselves

ring a bell.example: That name rings a bell — have I met her before?
ring a bell.tip: A bell ringing triggers a memory

the tip of the iceberg.meaning: Only a small part of a much larger issue
the tip of the iceberg.example: These problems are just the tip of the iceberg.
the tip of the iceberg.tip: Most of the iceberg is hidden underwater — the real problem is bigger

time flies.example: Time flies when you're having fun.
time flies.tip: Time zooming past like a bird in flight

to each his own.example: I wouldn't eat that, but to each his own.
to each his own.tip: Each person has their own taste — no judgment

turn over a new leaf.meaning: To start behaving in a better way
turn over a new leaf.example: After the incident, he turned over a new leaf.
turn over a new leaf.tip: Turning to a blank page — fresh start

burning bridges.meaning: To destroy a relationship or connection permanently
burning bridges.example: Don't burn bridges — you may need their help someday.
burning bridges.tip: Burning the bridge behind you — no way to go back

call it a day.example: We've done enough — let's call it a day.
call it a day.tip: Declaring the work day is over — time to stop

food for thought.example: Your idea about remote work is real food for thought.
food for thought.tip: Your mind 'eats' it — nourishment for thinking

get the ball rolling.meaning: To begin an activity or process
get the ball rolling.example: Let's get the ball rolling on the new project.
get the ball rolling.tip: A ball needs an initial push to start rolling

give the benefit of the doubt.meaning: To decide that someone is probably innocent or truthful
give the benefit of the doubt.example: I'll give her the benefit of the doubt this time.
give the benefit of the doubt.tip: When uncertain, choose to believe the better explanation

go down in flames.example: The product launch went down in flames.
go down in flames.tip: A burning plane crashing — dramatic failure

hit rock bottom.meaning: To reach the lowest possible point
hit rock bottom.example: After losing his job and home, he hit rock bottom.
hit rock bottom.tip: The very bottom of a mine shaft — can't go any lower

in hot water.meaning: In trouble; in a difficult situation
in hot water.example: He's in hot water with his parents after failing the test.
in hot water.tip: Being in boiling water — an uncomfortable, dangerous place

keep an eye on.example: Could you keep an eye on my bags while I'm away?
keep an eye on.tip: Your eye stays fixed on something — constant attention

know the ropes.meaning: To understand how something works; to be experienced
know the ropes.example: She really knows the ropes around here.
know the ropes.tip: Sailors who knew which ropes to pull — experienced crew

lose your touch.meaning: To lose an ability or skill you once had
lose your touch.example: He used to be a great cook, but he's lost his touch.

make ends meet.meaning: To have just enough money to live on
make ends meet.example: It's hard to make ends meet on a single income.
make ends meet.tip: Stretching your budget so the two ends barely touch

not my cup of tea.meaning: Not something you enjoy or are interested in
not my cup of tea.example: Horror movies are not my cup of tea.
not my cup of tea.tip: Tea you wouldn't choose to drink — not your preference

off the top of my head.example: Off the top of my head, I'd say about twenty.
off the top of my head.tip: The first thing that pops out of your head

put all your eggs in one basket.meaning: To risk everything on a single venture
put all your eggs in one basket.example: Don't put all your eggs in one basket — diversify your investments.
put all your eggs in one basket.tip: If the basket drops, ALL eggs break

scratch the surface.meaning: To deal with only a small part of a subject
scratch the surface.example: We've only scratched the surface of this issue.
scratch the surface.tip: Only touching the outer layer — not digging deep

take a rain check.meaning: To decline now but accept for another time
take a rain check.example: I can't come tonight — can I take a rain check?
take a rain check.tip: A rain check lets you use a ticket another day if it rains

the elephant in the room.meaning: An obvious problem that everyone ignores
the elephant in the room.example: Let's address the elephant in the room — we're over budget.
the elephant in the room.tip: An elephant is impossible to ignore, yet everyone pretends not to see it

throw caution to the wind.meaning: To act recklessly; to take a big risk
throw caution to the wind.example: She threw caution to the wind and quit her job to travel.
throw caution to the wind.tip: Tossing your careful warnings into the wind — letting them blow away

under someone's thumb.example: He's always been under his mother's thumb.
under someone's thumb.tip: Pinned down by a thumb — unable to move freely

weather the storm.example: The company weathered the storm of the recession.
weather the storm.tip: A ship enduring a storm — surviving through hardship

wrap up.tip: Tying up loose ends like wrapping a package

at the end of the day.example: At the end of the day, what matters is your happiness.
at the end of the day.tip: When the sun sets and you reflect on what really counts

give someone the cold shoulder.example: She gave him the cold shoulder after the argument.
give someone the cold shoulder.tip: Turning your cold shoulder toward someone — refusing warmth

in a nutshell.example: In a nutshell, the project failed due to lack of funding.
in a nutshell.tip: Everything compressed into a tiny nutshell — the shortest version

open a can of worms.meaning: To create a complicated situation by doing something
open a can of worms.example: Asking about his ex opened a real can of worms.
open a can of worms.tip: Opening the can releases worms everywhere — one action causes many problems

over the moon.example: She was over the moon when she got the promotion.
over the moon.tip: So happy you jump over the moon

rub salt in the wound.meaning: To make a bad situation even worse
rub salt in the wound.example: Don't rub salt in the wound by reminding me.
rub salt in the wound.tip: Salt on an open wound — literally adding pain

the whole nine yards.example: She decorated the house — balloons, streamers, the whole nine yards.
the whole nine yards.tip: All nine yards of fabric or ammunition — using every last bit

wild goose chase.example: Looking for that file was a wild goose chase.
wild goose chase.tip: Chasing wild geese — they fly away and you never catch them

a penny for your thoughts.meaning: Used to ask what someone is thinking about
a penny for your thoughts.example: You look thoughtful — a penny for your thoughts?
a penny for your thoughts.tip: Offering to pay a penny to hear someone's private thoughts

add fuel to the fire.meaning: To make an already bad situation worse
add fuel to the fire.example: His comments only added fuel to the fire.
add fuel to the fire.tip: Throwing fuel on flames — making them burn hotter

break a leg.example: You've got this — break a leg!
break a leg.tip: Theater superstition: saying 'good luck' is bad luck, so you say the opposite

cross that bridge when you come to it.meaning: To deal with a problem when it actually happens
cross that bridge when you come to it.example: We'll cross that bridge when we come to it.
cross that bridge when you come to it.tip: Don't worry about the bridge until you actually reach it

easier said than done.meaning: Something is more difficult in practice than it sounds
easier said than done.example: Losing weight is easier said than done.
easier said than done.tip: Words are easy; actions are hard

let sleeping dogs lie.meaning: To avoid interfering with a situation that might cause trouble
let sleeping dogs lie.example: Don't bring up the past — let sleeping dogs lie.
let sleeping dogs lie.tip: Waking a sleeping dog might get you bitten — leave it alone

the best of both worlds.meaning: Having the advantages of two different things at once
the best of both worlds.example: Working from home gives me the best of both worlds — comfort and career.
the best of both worlds.tip: Getting the good parts of two choices simultaneously

you can't judge a book by its cover.meaning: You shouldn't form an opinion based on appearance alone
you can't judge a book by its cover.example: He looks tough, but you can't judge a book by its cover.
you can't judge a book by its cover.tip: The cover reveals nothing about the story inside
